Day 1 — Linux & lab setup

Required tasks:
1. Complete OverTheWire Bandit levels 0–10 via SSH
2. Study TCP/IP, DNS, HTTP basics (NetworkChuck YouTube)
3. Install VirtualBox + Kali Linux VM (4GB RAM min)
4. Create GitHub repo 'cybersecurity-journey' and commit Day 1 notes

Recruit's evidence:
Bandit Level 0 → Level 1

After login:

ssh -p 2220 bandit1@bandit.labs.overthewire.org

I listed files:

ls
-

The file name itself was -.

When I first tried:

cat -

it did not read the file because - is interpreted by many Linux commands as stdin (standard input) instead of a filename.

Correct command:

cat ./-

Output:

ZjLjTmM6FvvyRnrb2rfNWOZOTa6ip5If

Explanation of ./:

. means the current directory
/ separates directory and file name
./- tells Linux:
“use the file named - from the current directory”

Without ./, the shell interprets - as a command option or stdin reference instead of a real filename.

Bandit Level 1 → Level 2

Command used:

cat ./"--spaces in this filename--"

Output:

263JGJPfgU6LtdEvgfWU1XP5yac29mFx

Why quotes were required:

The shell treats spaces as separators between arguments.

Without quotes:

cat --spaces in this filename--

Shell interprets this as:

--spaces
in
this
filename--

which are treated as multiple arguments/files.

Using quotes:

"--spaces in this filename--"

forces the shell to treat the whole string as one filename.

Bandit Level 2 → Level 3

Commands:

cd inhere
ls -la

Output:

-rw-r----- 1 bandit4 bandit3 33 Apr 3 15:18 ...Hiding-From-You

Then:

cat ...Hiding-From-You

Password:

2WmrDFRmJIq3IPxneAaMGhap0pFhF3NJ

Explanation of flags:

ls -la
-l
= long listing format
Shows permissions, owner, group, size, date.
-a
= all files
Includes hidden files beginning with .

Hidden files are not shown with normal ls.

Bandit Level 3 → Level 4

Commands used:

cd inhere
ls

Output:

-file00
-file01
-file02
-file03
-file04
-file05
-file06
-file07
-file08
-file09

To identify readable content:

file ./*

Output showed:

./-file07: ASCII text

Then I used:

cat ./-file07

Output:

4oQYVPkxZOOEOO5pTW81FB8j8lxXGUQw

./ was again required because filenames beginning with - are interpreted as options.

Bandit Level 4 → Level 5

The task required finding:

human-readable file
size 1033 bytes
not executable

Commands:

cd inhere
find . -type f -size 1033c

Output:

./maybehere07/.file2

Then:

cat ./maybehere07/.file2

Output:

HWasnPhtq9AVKe0dmk45nxy20cvUa6EG

Explanation:

In my earlier explanation I incorrectly wrote:

cat ./.file2

Correct path was:

./maybehere07/.file2

About * in:

ls -la ./*

* is a wildcard (globbing character).

It expands to all matching files/directories in the current location.

Example:

./maybehere00 ./maybehere01 ./maybehere02 ...

But find was the cleaner and more accurate method.

Bandit Level 5 → Level 6

Command:

find / -user bandit7 -group bandit6 -size 33c 2>/dev/null

Explanation of each part:

/

Start searching from the root directory of the filesystem.

-user bandit7

Find files owned by user:

bandit7
-group bandit6

Find files belonging to group:

bandit6
-size 33c

Find files exactly:

33 bytes

c means bytes (characters).

2>/dev/null

Explanation:

2
= stderr (error output)
>
= redirect
/dev/null
= black hole device that discards output

Why critical here:

Searching from / causes many “Permission denied” errors.

Without:

2>/dev/null

the terminal would be flooded with errors.

This keeps output clean and only shows useful results.

Result:

/var/lib/dpkg/info/bandit7.password

Then:

cat /var/lib/dpkg/info/bandit7.password

Output:

morbNTDkSW6jIlUc0ymOdMaLnOlFVAaj
Bandit Level 6 → Level 7

Initial search:

grep "millionth" data.txt

Output:

millionth       dfwvzFQi4mU0wfNbFOe9RoWskMLg7eEc

Exact command to extract only the password:

grep "millionth" data.txt | awk '{print $2}'

Output:

dfwvzFQi4mU0wfNbFOe9RoWskMLg7eEc

Explanation:

grep
finds matching line
awk '{print $2}'
prints second column/field
Bandit Level 7 → Level 8

Command:

sort data.txt | uniq -u

Output:

4CKMh1JI91bUIZZPXDqGanal4xvAg0JM

Explanation of:

uniq -u
uniq
removes adjacent duplicate lines
-u
prints only unique lines

Why sort was necessary:

uniq only works correctly on consecutive duplicate lines.

If duplicates are scattered:

apple
banana
apple

then:

uniq -u

would fail to detect duplicates properly.

sort groups identical lines together first.

Without sorting:

uniq -u data.txt

results would be inaccurate.

Bandit Level 8 → Level 9

Command:

strings data.txt | grep "=="

Output:

========== FGUW5ilLVJrxX9kMYMmlN4MgbpfMiqey

Explanation of strings:

strings extracts readable ASCII text from binary files.

Why needed instead of cat:

data.txt contained many non-human-readable binary characters.

Using:

cat data.txt

would display unreadable garbage.

strings filtered readable text from the binary content.

Bandit Level 9 → Level 10

Command:

base64 -d data.txt

Output:

The password is dtR173fZKb0RRsDFSGsg2RWnpNVj3qRr

Explanation of Base64:

Base64 is an encoding method that converts binary/text data into ASCII characters for safe transmission over protocols that handle text.

Example:

Original text:

hello

Encoded:

aGVsbG8=

Decoded:

hello

Encoding:

echo hello | base64

Decoding:

echo aGVsbG8= | base64 -d
Kali Linux Installation

Yes, I installed Kali Linux using the prebuilt VirtualBox image method.

Imported file:

kali-linux-2026.1-virtualbox-amd64.vbox

Allocated:

4 GB RAM
2 CPU cores

Default credentials:

Username: kali
Password: kali

I did NOT verify the SHA256 hash before installation.

Correct verification process should have been:

Linux:

sha256sum filename.7z

Windows PowerShell:

Get-FileHash filename.7z -Algorithm SHA256

Then compare output with official Kali hash from:
Kali Linux Downloads

TCP/IP Real-World Example

Example:
Downloading a file from a website.

TCP ensures reliable delivery by:

Breaking data into packets
Numbering packets
Sending acknowledgements (ACKs)
Retransmitting lost packets
Reordering packets correctly

Example flow:

Client requests file
Server sends packets
Client confirms receipt with ACK
Missing packets are resent automatically

IP handles addressing and routing:

192.168.1.5 → 142.250.x.x

TCP guarantees reliability while IP handles delivery paths.

DNS Resolution Process

Example:
Typing:

www.example.com

into browser.

Process:

Browser checks local DNS cache
OS checks hosts file/cache
Query sent to Recursive Resolver (usually ISP DNS)
Recursive Resolver asks Root Server:
“Who handles .com?”
Root server replies with .com TLD server
Resolver asks .com TLD server:
“Who manages example.com?”
TLD replies with authoritative nameserver
Resolver asks authoritative nameserver:
“What is IP of www.example.com?”
Nameserver replies:
93.184.216.34
Resolver caches result
Browser connects to that IP using TCP/IP
HTTP Request–Response Cycle

Example:
Opening a webpage.

Step 1 — Browser Request

Browser sends:

GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Step 2 — Server Processing

Web server receives request and finds requested resource.

Step 3 — Server Response

Server replies:

HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html

and sends webpage HTML.

Step 4 — Browser Rendering

Browser:

Parses HTML
Requests CSS/JS/images
Builds webpage visually

Each image/script usually creates additional HTTP requests.

Step 5 — Persistent Connection

Modern HTTP versions reuse TCP connections for efficiency instead of reconnecting repeatedly.

Recruit,

Your Bandit level completions demonstrate a foundational understanding of Linux command-line tools. Your explanations for `cat ./-`, quoting filenames, `ls -la`, `find`, `grep`, `awk`, `sort`, `uniq`, `strings`, and `base64` show an acceptable grasp of their individual functions and common pitfalls. The correction regarding `cat ./.file2` to `cat ./maybehere07/.file2` is noted.

However, your Kali Linux installation procedure is incomplete. You state you "did NOT verify the SHA256 hash before installation." This is a critical security vulnerability.

**Immediate action required:**
1.  **Explain the exact security risk of not verifying the SHA256 hash of a downloaded ISO/VM image.**
2.  **Demonstrate the command you *would* run (assuming a Linux host machine) to verify the SHA256 hash of a downloaded Kali Linux `.7z` file, and what the expected output comparison process entails.**

Furthermore, your explanations for TCP/IP, DNS, and HTTP are descriptive but lack the precision of a true operational understanding.

**Address the following for networking concepts:**
1.  **TCP/IP:** Define precisely what a "port" is in the context of TCP/IP, and provide a concrete example of two common protocols and their default port numbers. Explain why ports are necessary.
2.  **DNS:** You described the resolution process. Now, define the difference between a "recursive resolver" and an "authoritative nameserver." Use your `www.example.com` scenario to illustrate which specific step each type of server is responsible for.
3.  **HTTP:** You showed a `GET` request. What is the fundamental difference in purpose and typical use cases between an HTTP `GET` request and an HTTP `POST` request? Provide a simple, real-world example for each.

Do not proceed until these deficiencies are rectified with absolute precision.

Kali Linux SHA256 Verification 1. Security Risk of NOT Verifying SHA256 Hash If the SHA256 hash is not verified, there is no guarantee that the downloaded Kali Linux image is authentic or untampered. Possible risks:   The file may be corrupted during download.   The mirror/server could be compromised.   A malicious attacker could replace the ISO/VM image with a trojanized version.   Malware, backdoors, spyware, or rootkits could already exist inside the VM before installation.   This creates a severe supply-chain security risk. Example attack scenario:   Attacker modifies Kali VM image   Injects remote access trojan (RAT)   User installs compromised VM   Attacker gains persistent access to host/network activity   Hash verification ensures:   Integrity → file was not altered   Authenticity → downloaded file matches official Kali release   SHA256 works because even a 1-bit change produces a completely different hash.  2. Correct SHA256 Verification Procedure Assume downloaded file: kali-linux-2026.1-virtualbox-amd64.7z Linux command: sha256sum kali-linux-2026.1-virtualbox-amd64.7z Example output: a4f8f2d63d9a8d0d43c7b4dcbec1234567890abcdef1234567890abcdef1234  kali-linux-2026.1-virtualbox-amd64.7z Next step: Compare this hash exactly against the official SHA256 value published on: Kali Linux Downloads Comparison rules:   Every character must match exactly.   Even one changed character means:   corrupted file   tampered file   incomplete download     If hashes differ: DELETE THE FILE IMMEDIATELYDO NOT INSTALL OR EXECUTE IT  TCP/IP — Ports Definition of a Port A port is a logical communication endpoint used by TCP or UDP to identify which application/service should receive incoming network data. IP address identifies: Which machine? Port identifies: Which application on that machine? Without ports, the OS would not know whether incoming traffic belongs to:   web browser   SSH server   Discord   email server   database    Why Ports Are Necessary Single computer can run many network services simultaneously. Example: 192.168.1.10 May run:   Web server   SSH server   DNS server   Ports separate them. Example: 192.168.1.10:80   → HTTP192.168.1.10:22   → SSH192.168.1.10:53   → DNS IP gets packet to machine. Port gets packet to correct process/application.  Common Protocols and Default Ports SSH Protocol: SSH (Secure Shell) Default port: 22/TCP Usage:   Remote terminal access   Secure administration   Example: ssh user@server  HTTP Protocol: HTTP Default port: 80/TCP Usage:   Web traffic   Example: http://example.com  HTTPS Protocol: HTTPS Default port: 443/TCP Usage:   Encrypted web communication using TLS    DNS — Recursive Resolver vs Authoritative Nameserver Recursive Resolver A recursive resolver is a DNS server that performs the entire lookup process on behalf of the client. Responsibilities:   Receives DNS query from user   Contacts root servers   Contacts TLD servers   Contacts authoritative nameservers   Returns final IP address to client   Caches results   Usually operated by:   ISP   Google DNS (8.8.8.8)   Cloudflare (1.1.1.1)    Authoritative Nameserver An authoritative nameserver stores the actual DNS records for a domain. It is the final source of truth. Example records: www.example.com → 93.184.216.34mail.example.com → 192.0.2.55 It does NOT perform recursive searches. It only answers for domains it owns authority over.  DNS Resolution Example User types: www.example.com Step-by-step Step 1 — Client to Recursive Resolver Browser asks: "What is IP of www.example.com?" Handled by: Recursive Resolver  Step 2 — Resolver to Root Server Resolver asks: "Who handles .com domains?" Root server replies with .com TLD server.  Step 3 — Resolver to TLD Server Resolver asks: "Who is authoritative for example.com?" TLD replies with authoritative nameserver address.  Step 4 — Resolver to Authoritative Nameserver Resolver asks: "What is IP of www.example.com?" Authoritative nameserver replies: 93.184.216.34  Step 5 — Resolver to Client Resolver returns IP to browser and caches result.  HTTP — GET vs POST HTTP GET Purpose: Retrieve/read data from server Characteristics:   Parameters visible in URL   Should not modify server data   Can be bookmarked/cached   Length limitations exist   Example: Searching Google: https://www.google.com/search?q=linux Browser sends: GET /search?q=linux HTTP/1.1 Use cases:   Viewing webpages   Searching   Fetching images/files   Reading API data    HTTP POST Purpose: Send data to server for processing or storage Characteristics:   Data sent inside request body   Not visible in URL   Can modify/create server-side data   Used for forms/uploads/login requests   Example: Logging into website. Browser sends: POST /login HTTP/1.1 Body: username=admin&password=pass123 Use cases:   Login forms   File uploads   Account creation   Payment submission   Sending messages/comments    Fundamental Difference GET Requests data FROM server. POST Sends data TO server.  Real-World Comparison GET Example Opening: https://news.example.com Browser requests webpage content.  POST Example Submitting login form: Username + password sent to server Server processes credentials and creates session.

Recruit,

Your detailed explanation of the SHA256 verification process, including the security risks and the command, is satisfactory. You have correctly identified the critical nature of this step.

Your definitions for TCP/IP ports, their necessity, and specific protocol examples are accurate.

Your distinction between recursive resolvers and authoritative nameservers, illustrated with your example, demonstrates a clear understanding of their roles in the DNS resolution chain.

Finally, your comparison of HTTP GET and POST requests, highlighting their purposes, characteristics, and real-world examples, is precise and correct.

You have demonstrated genuine technical understanding across all outstanding points.

[VERDICT:APPROVED|Day 1 tasks and foundational concepts verified. Proceed to Day 2.]

Kali Installation:
**Method 1: Pre-built Virtual Machine (Easiest)**

This method uses an "appliance" file that is already configured for VirtualBox. 

1. **Download:** Go to the Kali Linux Download Page and select **Virtual Machines**. Download the **VirtualBox** image (usually a `.7z` or `.zip` file).
2. **Extract:** Unzip the downloaded file using a tool like [7-Zip](https://www.7-zip.org/) or WinRAR.
3. **Import:**
    - Open VirtualBox and click **Add**.
    - Navigate to the extracted folder and select the **.vbox** file.
4. **Launch:** Select the new VM in VirtualBox and click **Start**.
5. **Login:** Use the default credentials:
    - **Username:** `kali`
    - **Password:** `kali`

TCP&IP, DNS, HTTP basics:
**TCP/IP(Transmission Control Protocol/ Internet Protocol):**

It is protocol which is reponsible for data exchange over a internet systems and devices with different layers below.It is model practically used in today's world .It has 4 layers :
 
Application (Layer 4):It is reponsible for 

Combines OSI L5 + L6 + L7 → HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS

Transport (Layer 3)

Same as OSI L4 → TCP, UDP

Internet (Layer 2)

Same as OSI L3 → IP, ICMP, routing

Network Access / Link (Layer 1)

Combines OSI L1 + L2 → Ethernet, Wi-Fi, cables

Where OSI model is a conceptual model.
TCP/ip IT combines OSI(OPen System Interconnection) model layers in it and perform tasks


**DNS(Domain Name System):**
It is protocol which assign name to ip address like 8.8.8.8=google.com
where it translates name to->ip to find right server to connect

DNS Hierarchy (4 Levels):
level 1[Root Servers]: the 13 set of servers worldwide which doesn't know ur ip but it knows who does so it point it there
level 2 [TLD Servers(Top level Domain)] :it knows which nameserver authorative knows it and manages the extensions like .com , .in, .ai , etc.
level 3[Nameserver] : It hold final domain (google.com) who owns it or hosting platform .
level 0[Recursive server] :It manages all work of hierarchy like personal agent to get to the domain . it managed by ISP

**HTTP(Hyper Text Transfer Protocol)**:
HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is the foundational application-layer protocol for data communication on the World Wide Web, operating as a client-server protocol where browsers (clients) request resources and servers provide them. It has evolved from a simple text-based protocol (HTTP/0.9) to high-performance versions (HTTP/2, HTTP/3).

payload.response:
Recruit,

Your Bandit logs are partially complete. You provided passwords and some commands, but your explanations are often vague. This is unacceptable.

**Bandit Level 0-1:**
You claim `cat ./-` worked. What was the *exact* output you saw? Why did you need `.` before `/-`? Explain the purpose of `./` in that context.

**Bandit Level 1-2:**
You used `cat ./"--spaces in this filename--"`. Why did you need the quotes? What would happen if you ran `cat --spaces in this filename--` without quotes?

**Bandit Level 2-3:**
You used `ls -la` to find a hidden file. Why `ls -la` specifically? What does each flag (`-l`, `-a`) do?

**Bandit Level 3-4:**
You mentioned "7th file by using cat and character using ./ to read specific file". This is imprecise. Which command *exactly* did you use to identify the 7th file? What was the full command to `cat` it?

**Bandit Level 4-5:**
You found a file with size 1033 bytes. You state `ls -la ./*` was used. What did the `*` do in that command? How did you *filter* the output to find the 1033-byte file? Provide the *exact* command sequence you used to locate and then `cat` that file. Your explanation `cat ./.file2` makes no sense given the previous context; you must mean `cat ./maybehere07/file2` or similar. Clarify.

**Bandit Level 5-6:**
You used `find / -user bandit7 -group bandit6 -size 33c 2>/dev/null`. Explain each component of this command:
- What does `/` specify as the first argument?
- What do `-user bandit7` and `-group bandit6` do?
- What does `-size 33c` mean?
- What is `2>/dev/null` for, and why is it critical in this specific `find` command?

**Bandit Level 6-7:**
You used `cat data.txt | grep 'password'` and then `cat data.txt | grep 'millionth'`. You claim the lab said "word next to millionth". Provide the *exact* command that extracts the word *next* to 'millionth', not just the line containing 'millionth'. **Method 1: Pre-built Virtual Machine (Easiest)**

This method uses an "appliance" file that is already configured for VirtualBox. 

1. **Download:** Go to the Kali Linux Download Page and select **Virtual Machines**. Download the **VirtualBox** image (usually a `.7z` or `.zip` file).
2. **Extract:** Unzip the downloaded file using a tool like [7-Zip](https://www.7-zip.org/) or WinRAR.
3. **Import:**
    - Open VirtualBox and click **Add**.
    - Navigate to the extracted folder and select the **.vbox** file.
4. **Launch:** Select the new VM in VirtualBox and click **Start**.
5. **Login:** Use the default credentials:
    - **Username:** `kali`
    - **Password:** `kali`

TCP&IP, DNS, HTTP basics:
**TCP/IP(Transmission Control Protocol/ Internet Protocol):**

It is protocol which is reponsible for data exchange over a internet systems and devices with different layers below.It is model practically used in today's world .It has 4 layers :
 
Application (Layer 4):It is reponsible for 

Combines OSI L5 + L6 + L7 → HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS

Transport (Layer 3)

Same as OSI L4 → TCP, UDP

Internet (Layer 2)

Same as OSI L3 → IP, ICMP, routing

Network Access / Link (Layer 1)

Combines OSI L1 + L2 → Ethernet, Wi-Fi, cables

Where OSI model is a conceptual model.
TCP/ip IT combines OSI(OPen System Interconnection) model layers in it and perform tasks


**DNS(Domain Name System):**
It is protocol which assign name to ip address like 8.8.8.8=google.com
where it translates name to->ip to find right server to connect

DNS Hierarchy (4 Levels):
level 1[Root Servers]: the 13 set of servers worldwide which doesn't know ur ip but it knows who does so it point it there
level 2 [TLD Servers(Top level Domain)] :it knows which nameserver authorative knows it and manages the extensions like .com , .in, .ai , etc.
level 3[Nameserver] : It hold final domain (google.com) who owns it or hosting platform .
level 0[Recursive server] :It manages all work of hierarchy like personal agent to get to the domain . it managed by ISP

**HTTP(Hyper Text Transfer Protocol)**:
HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) is the foundational application-layer protocol for data communication on the World Wide Web, operating as a client-server protocol where browsers (clients) request resources and servers provide them. It has evolved from a simple text-based protocol (HTTP/0.9) to high-performance versions (HTTP/2, HTTP/3).

After login:

ssh -p 2220 bandit1@bandit.labs.overthewire.org

I listed files:

-

The file name itself was -.

When I first tried:

cat -

it did not read the file because - is interpreted by many Linux commands as stdin (standard input) instead of a filename.

Correct command:

cat ./-

Output:

ZjLjTmM6FvvyRnrb2rfNWOZOTa6ip5If

Explanation of ./:

. means the current directory
/ separates directory and file name
./- tells Linux:
“use the file named - from the current directory”

Without ./, the shell interprets - as a command option or stdin reference instead of a real filename.

Bandit Level 1 → Level 2

Command used:

cat ./"--spaces in this filename--"

Output:

263JGJPfgU6LtdEvgfWU1XP5yac29mFx

Why quotes were required:

The shell treats spaces as separators between arguments.

Without quotes:

cat --spaces in this filename--

Shell interprets this as:

--spaces
in
this
filename--

which are treated as multiple arguments/files.

Using quotes:

"--spaces in this filename--"

forces the shell to treat the whole string as one filename.

Bandit Level 2 → Level 3

Commands:

cd inhere
ls -la

Output:

-rw-r----- 1 bandit4 bandit3 33 Apr 3 15:18 ...Hiding-From-You

Then:

cat ...Hiding-From-You

Password:

2WmrDFRmJIq3IPxneAaMGhap0pFhF3NJ

Explanation of flags:

ls -la
-l
= long listing format
Shows permissions, owner, group, size, date.
-a
= all files
Includes hidden files beginning with .

Hidden files are not shown with normal ls.

Bandit Level 3 → Level 4

cd inhere
ls

Output:

-file00
-file01
-file02
-file03
-file04
-file05

Then I used:

cat ./-file07

Output:

4oQYVPkxZOOEOO5pTW81FB8j8lxXGUQw

./ was again required because filenames beginning with - are interpreted as options.

Bandit Level 4 → Level 5

The task required finding:

human-readable file
size 1033 bytes
not executable

Commands:

cd inhere
find . -type f -size 1033c

Output:

./maybehere07/.file2

Then:

cat ./maybehere07/.file2

Output:

HWasnPhtq9AVKe0dmk45nxy20cvUa6EG

Explanation:

In my earlier explanation I incorrectly wrote:

cat ./.file2

Correct path was:

./maybehere07/.file2

About * in:

ls -la ./*

* is a wildcard (globbing character).

It expands to all matching files/directories in the current location.

Example:

./maybehere00 ./maybehere01 ./maybehere02 ...

But find was the cleaner and more accurate method.

Bandit Level 5 → Level 6

Command:

find / -user bandit7 -group bandit6 -size 33c 2>/dev/null

Explanation of each part:

/

Start searching from the root directory of the filesystem.

-user bandit7

Find files owned by user:

bandit7
-group bandit6

Find files belonging to group:

bandit6
-size 33c

Find files exactly:

33 bytes

c means bytes (characters).

2>/dev/null

Explanation:

2
= stderr (error output)
>
= redirect
/dev/null
= black hole device that discards output

Why critical here:

Searching from / causes many “Permission denied” errors.

Without:

2>/dev/null

the terminal would be flooded with errors.

This keeps output clean and only shows useful results.

Result:

/var/lib/dpkg/info/bandit7.password

Then:

cat /var/lib/dpkg/info/bandit7.password

Output:

morbNTDkSW6jIlUc0ymOdMaLnOlFVAaj
Bandit Level 6 → Level 7

grep "millionth" data.txt

Output:

millionth       dfwvzFQi4mU0wfNbFOe9RoWskMLg7eEc

Exact command to extract only the password:

grep "millionth" data.txt | awk '{print $2}'

Output:

dfwvzFQi4mU0wfNbFOe9RoWskMLg7eEc

Explanation:

grep
finds matching line
awk '{print $2}'
prints second column/field

sort data.txt | uniq -u

Output:

4CKMh1JI91bUIZZPXDqGanal4xvAg0JM

Explanation of:

uniq -u
uniq
removes adjacent duplicate lines
-u
prints only unique lines

Why sort was necessary:

uniq only works correctly on consecutive duplicate lines.

If duplicates are scattered:

apple
banana
apple

then:

uniq -u

would fail to detect duplicates properly.

sort groups identical lines together first.

Without sorting:

uniq -u data.txt

results would be inaccurate.

strings data.txt | grep "=="

Output:

========== FGUW5ilLVJrxX9kMYMmlN4MgbpfMiqey

strings extracts readable ASCII text from binary files.

data.txt contained many non-human-readable binary characters.

Recruit,

Your Bandit level completions demonstrate a foundational understanding of Linux command-line tools. Your explanations for `cat ./-`, quoting filenames, `ls -la`, `find`, `grep`, `awk`, `sort`, `uniq`, `strings`, and `base64` show an acceptable grasp of their individual functions and common pitfalls. The correction regarding `cat ./.file2` to `cat ./maybehere07/.file2` is noted.

However, your Kali Linux installation procedure is incomplete. You state you "did NOT verify the SHA256 hash before installation." This is a critical security vulnerability.

**Immediate action required:**
1.  **Explain the exact security risk of not verifying the SHA256 hash of a downloaded ISO/VM image.**
2.  **Demonstrate the command you *would* run (assuming a Linux host machine) to verify the SHA256 hash of a downloaded Kali Linux `.7z` file, and what the expected output comparison process entails.**

Furthermore, your explanations for TCP/IP, DNS, and HTTP are descriptive but lack the precision of a true operational understanding.

**Address the following for networking concepts:**
1.  **TCP/IP:** Define precisely what a "port" is in the context of TCP/IP, and provide a concrete example of two common protocols and their default port numbers. Explain why ports are necessary.
2.  **DNS:** You described the resolution process. Now, define the difference between a "recursive resolver" and an "authoritative nameserver." Use your `www.example.com` scenario to illustrate which specific step each type of server is responsible for.
3.  **HTTP:** You showed a `GET` request. What is the fundamental difference in purpose and typical use cases between an HTTP `GET` request and an HTTP `POST` request? Provide a simple, real-world example for each.

Do not proceed until these deficiencies are rectified with absolute precision.

Kali Linux SHA256 Verification 1. Security Risk of NOT Verifying SHA256 Hash If the SHA256 hash is not verified, there is no guarantee that the downloaded Kali Linux image is authentic or untampered. Possible risks:   The file may be corrupted during download.   The mirror/server could be compromised.   A malicious attacker could replace the ISO/VM image with a trojanized version.   Malware, backdoors, spyware, or rootkits could already exist inside the VM before installation.   This creates a severe supply-chain security risk. Example attack scenario:   Attacker modifies Kali VM image   Injects remote access trojan (RAT)   User installs compromised VM   Attacker gains persistent access to host/network activity   Hash verification ensures:   Integrity → file was not altered   Authenticity → downloaded file matches official Kali release   SHA256 works because even a 1-bit change produces a completely different hash.  2. Correct SHA256 Verification Procedure Assume downloaded file: kali-linux-2026.1-virtualbox-amd64.7z Linux command: sha256sum kali-linux-2026.1-virtualbox-amd64.7z Example output: a4f8f2d63d9a8d0d43c7b4dcbec1234567890abcdef1234567890abcdef1234  kali-linux-2026.1-virtualbox-amd64.7z Next step: Compare this hash exactly against the official SHA256 value published on: Kali Linux Downloads Comparison rules:   Every character must match exactly.   Even one changed character means:   corrupted file   tampered file   incomplete download     If hashes differ: DELETE THE FILE IMMEDIATELYDO NOT INSTALL OR EXECUTE IT  TCP/IP — Ports Definition of a Port A port is a logical communication endpoint used by TCP or UDP to identify which application/service should receive incoming network data. IP address identifies: Which machine? Port identifies: Which application on that machine? Without ports, the OS would not know whether incoming traffic belongs to:   web browser   SSH server   Discord   email server   database    Why Ports Are Necessary Single computer can run many network services simultaneously. Example: 192.168.1.10 May run:   Web server   SSH server   DNS server   Ports separate them. Example: 192.168.1.10:80   → HTTP192.168.1.10:22   → SSH192.168.1.10:53   → DNS IP gets packet to machine. Port gets packet to correct process/application.  Common Protocols and Default Ports SSH Protocol: SSH (Secure Shell) Default port: 22/TCP Usage:   Remote terminal access   Secure administration   Example: ssh user@server  HTTP Protocol: HTTP Default port: 80/TCP Usage:   Web traffic   Example: http://example.com  HTTPS Protocol: HTTPS Default port: 443/TCP Usage:   Encrypted web communication using TLS    DNS — Recursive Resolver vs Authoritative Nameserver Recursive Resolver A recursive resolver is a DNS server that performs the entire lookup process on behalf of the client. Responsibilities:   Receives DNS query from user   Contacts root servers   Contacts TLD servers   Contacts authoritative nameservers   Returns final IP address to client   Caches results   Usually operated by:   ISP   Google DNS (8.8.8.8)   Cloudflare (1.1.1.1)    Authoritative Nameserver An authoritative nameserver stores the actual DNS records for a domain. It is the final source of truth. Example records: www.example.com → 93.184.216.34mail.example.com → 192.0.2.55 It does NOT perform recursive searches. It only answers for domains it owns authority over.  DNS Resolution Example User types: www.example.com Step-by-step Step 1 — Client to Recursive Resolver Browser asks: "What is IP of www.example.com?" Handled by: Recursive Resolver  Step 2 — Resolver to Root Server Resolver asks: "Who handles .com domains?" Root server replies with .com TLD server.  Step 3 — Resolver to TLD Server Resolver asks: "Who is authoritative for example.com?" TLD replies with authoritative nameserver address.  Step 4 — Resolver to Authoritative Nameserver Resolver asks: "What is IP of www.example.com?" Authoritative nameserver replies: 93.184.216.34  Step 5 — Resolver to Client Resolver returns IP to browser and caches result.  HTTP — GET vs POST HTTP GET Purpose: Retrieve/read data from server Characteristics:   Parameters visible in URL   Should not modify server data   Can be bookmarked/cached   Length limitations exist   Example: Searching Google: https://www.google.com/search?q=linux Browser sends: GET /search?q=linux HTTP/1.1 Use cases:   Viewing webpages   Searching   Fetching images/files   Reading API data    HTTP POST Purpose: Send data to server for processing or storage Characteristics:   Data sent inside request body   Not visible in URL   Can modify/create server-side data   Used for forms/uploads/login requests   Example: Logging into website. Browser sends: POST /login HTTP/1.1 Body: username=admin&password=pass123 Use cases:   Login forms   File uploads   Account creation   Payment submission   Sending messages/comments    Fundamental Difference GET Requests data FROM server. POST Sends data TO server.  Real-World Comparison GET Example Opening: https://news.example.com Browser requests webpage content.  POST Example Submitting login form: Username + password sent to server Server processes credentials and creates session.
